funFact: Great white sharks can detect a single drop of blood diluted across 25 gallons of water, and sense the faint electrical fields produced by beating hearts.
description: The great white shark is the world's largest predatory fish, found in cool coastal and offshore waters across the globe. Despite their fearsome reputation, attacks on humans are rare and typically cases of mistaken identity — they strongly prefer fat-rich marine mammals over people.
habitat: Cool coastal and offshore waters globally; coastal and offshore waters of continental and insular shelves, shallow bays, and near pinniped colonies. Recorded at depths to 4,199 ft (1,280 m).
geographicRangeDescription: Found in temperate and cool coastal waters across all major ocean basins. Key populations exist off South Africa, southern Australia, New Zealand, the northeastern United States, and California. Also present in the Mediterranean, the Azores, and parts of the western Indian Ocean. While capable of crossing open ocean, they favor productive coastal zones near pinniped colonies.
diet: Juveniles feed primarily on fish; adults shift to marine mammals — seals, sea lions, and dolphins — as well as large fish, rays, and sea turtles.
size: Males mature at ~7.8 ft (2.4 m); max confirmed 20.9 ft (6.4 m), possibly exceeding 26 ft (8 m).

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Seals, sea lions, dolphins, large fish, rays & sea turtles (adults)

vitalSigns.label: Key Senses
vitalSigns.value: Electroreception (Ampullae of Lorenzini); olfaction detects 1 drop of blood in 100 L of water

populationTrendMeta.note: Modeled from a 2011 NE Pacific mark-recapture study (Chapple et al.) extrapolated globally. The ~3,500 adult figure is the most-cited estimate but is widely acknowledged as uncertain.

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Bluefin tuna collapse; ~97% fish biomass lost

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Seal and herring stocks heavily depleted

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Africa
preyDeclineRegions.note: Cape fur seal colonies under moderate pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sea lion and tuna populations more stable

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense longline and gillnet fleets; minimal enforcement

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Incidental bycatch in swordfish and tuna longlines

fishingPressureRegions.name: Australia
fishingPressureRegions.note: Government-sanctioned drum lines and shark nets

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Illegal targeted fishing for jaws and fins

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Africa
fishingPressureRegions.note: Shark nets at beaches; ongoing bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Lower commercial fishing pressure in open waters

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES Appendix II, some EEZs

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: Caught unintentionally in longlines, gill nets, and drum lines set for other species. The leading cause of mortality globally.

threats.name: Targeted Poaching
threats.description: Hunted for jaws, teeth, fins, and liver oil. Trophy jaws can fetch tens of thousands of dollars on the black market.

threats.name: Habitat Degradation
threats.description: Coastal development, pollution, and noise reduce the quality of pupping and feeding grounds near seal colonies.

threats.name: Prey Depletion
threats.description: Overfishing of pinnipeds and large fish reduces the food base, forcing sharks to travel further and expend more energy.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming oceans shift prey distributions, alter migration routes, and may affect juvenile survival in pupping areas.

actionItems.title: Support Shark Conservation Organizations
actionItems.description: Donate to or volunteer with groups like Shark Trust, Save Our Seas Foundation, and Shark Research Institute that fund research, advocacy, and education.

actionItems.title: Advocate for Stronger Bycatch Regulations
actionItems.description: Contact your representatives to support legislation requiring circle hooks, excluder devices, and observer programs on commercial fishing vessels.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Use guides from the Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch program. Avoiding unsustainable fisheries reduces bycatch pressure on shark populations.

actionItems.title: Oppose Shark Finning
actionItems.description: Refuse to purchase or consume shark fin products, and support restaurants and markets that have pledged not to carry them.

actionItems.title: Spread Accurate Information
actionItems.description: Challenge fear-based media portrayals. Sharing science-based facts about shark behavior helps build public support for conservation.
